source: a16z Podcast — Is there an AI bubble?
summary: Legacy SaaS companies with profitable existing businesses should fund AI products at breakeven to compete — few are doing this
summary_extended: Baker expresses surprise that legacy public SaaS companies with profitable existing businesses have not used those cash flows to cross-subsidize AI product development at breakeven, arguing this is a viable and underutilized competitive strategy. He singles out public coding companies as a specific example, questioning why none of them are even attempting to compete with Cursor, an AI-native coding tool that is rapidly accumulating what Baker describes as a trillion tokens of coding data — a dataset he believes will become a durable, compounding competitive moat. Baker implies the window to compete remains open but is closing, and that incumbents who act now could still mount a credible challenge. The investment implication is cautionary for legacy SaaS incumbents who fail to act: they risk ceding the AI application layer to nimble, data-rich upstarts like Cursor, while those who do cross-subsidize aggressively may preserve long-term relevance.
detail: Baker is surprised more public SaaS companies haven't used their profitable legacy businesses to cross-subsidize AI product development at breakeven. He specifically calls out public coding companies for not competing with Cursor, noting Cursor is accumulating a trillion tokens of coding data that will become a durable moat. The window to compete is still open but closing.
confidence_evidence: Baker expresses surprise and frames it as an opportunity, but acknowledges Martin (in audience) is skeptical and calls it 'a chance' not a certainty
quote_fragment: why are none of the public coding companies even trying to compete with cursor
